text:

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Summer, summer, summertime ☀️

June 21 is the summer solstice for folks north of the Equator. This date is when the Northern Hemisphere reaches its maximum tilt toward the Sun and has its longest stretch of daylight all year.

In the Southern Hemisphere, the opposite is true! June 21 is the start of winter and the shortest day for friends south of the Equator. https://go.nasa.gov/3ScUvlu

media.ocrText: The last rays of an orbital sunset shine through Earth’s limb. Its bright rays illuminate Earth’s atmosphere in blue and orange and outline a line of clouds. Credit: NASA/Jessica Meir

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
How do you run a marathon on Mars? Ask our Perseverance rover!

Perseverance has now traveled 26.2 miles across the Red Planet since it first touched down in Jezero Crater in 2021. For the last five years, the rover has explored the Martian surface for signs of ancient life—and it's made some exciting discoveries.

Learn more about Perseverance and its mission: https://science.nasa.gov/mission/mars-2020-perseverance/
Follow an interactive map of Perseverance's journey: https://science.nasa.gov/mission/mars-2020-perseverance/location-map/

media.ocrText: A graphic visualizing Perseverance's journey across Mars, overlaid with a bumper-sticker type graphic with "26.2" in large white text against a reddish background. Credit: NASA

topComments.text: I have had four people read my shirt that reads "I climbed Olympus Mons 69,841'" and they ask me: "When did you climb Olympus Mons?" People need to brush up on their Martian geography. Does anyone know how much $1 trillion weighs?

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
This might look like a swarm of lightning bugs — but it's actually a cluster of galaxies, each containing countless stars.

Why is there a bubble in the middle? The galaxy cluster is so massive that it literally warps space and time, creating a cosmic magnifying glass known as gravitational lensing. Astronomers use these lenses to study objects in space that would otherwise be too distant to be seen.

media.ocrText: A scene strewn with galaxies of various colors. Some are clearly defined, while others are merely bright points. Image credit: NASA, ESA, M. Postman (STScI); Image Processing: G. Kober (NASA/Catholic University of America)

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Something super in our neighborhood 💫

Using data from our Chandra X-ray Observatory, scientists have discovered possible remains of a supernova in the middle of our Milky Way galaxy, 26,000 light-years away from Earth.

If confirmed, this supernova piece would be one of the closest to our galaxy’s central black hole that we have ever found. https://go.nasa.gov/4uFP6ks

media.ocrText: A photo of the site where the potential remains of a supernova. The photo shows an amorphous blob of red light with a spot of blue in the lower center. The background is a diffuse star field in black space. Credit: X-ray: NASA/CXC/UCLA/Z. Zhu et al.; ESA/XMM-Newton; Optical: PanSTARRS; Radio: MeerKAT; Infrared (JWST): NASA/ESA/CSA/STScI; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/L. Frattare and P. Edmonds

topComments.text: I guess it's still possible to receive a signal(s) from an advanced extra-terrestrial civilizations though from the distant past.  Or from another galaxy, the nearest 25,000 light years away.   That's a long time ago.

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Creators: Our Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is launching this summer, and you’re invited!

We're looking for storytellers to join us as our Roman Space Telescope lifts off to help answer some of the biggest questions about our universe. Apply by Sunday, June 28 at 11:59 p.m. EDT (0359 UTC June 29): https://go.nasa.gov/4vfHSVk

media.ocrText: A technician wearing white clean room protective attire installing Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope's solar array Sun shield — a set of solar panels on the telescope. The panels are dark with red outlines. Credit: NASA

topComments.text: I see they brought in Tom Cruise for the final adjustments. LOL. Backtothemoon Martin

NASA Aeronautics

sharedPost.text:
✈️ ANOTHER MILESTONE UNLOCKED: NASA’s X-59 reaches speed, altitude for future quiet supersonic flight testing!

NASA's X-59 experimental aircraft achieved a major milestone June 12, reaching Mach 1.4 (about 924 mph) and an altitude of 55,000 feet – its fastest and highest flight so far, and the conditions required for future mission-critical tests.

These flights are focused on performance, the quiet flights are coming soon. After acoustic validation, NASA’s Quesst mission will fly the aircraft over several U.S. communities 🏘️ to collect data on public perception of the quieter sonic thump the aircraft will make at supersonic speeds. 🔊 ✈️

Learn more: https://go.nasa.gov/4av0GHH

 #NASA #X59 #Quesst

media.ocrText: NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft reached its target speed and altitude for future community overflights for the first time during a flight on Friday, June 12, 2026. The milestone marked the first time the aircraft flew at Mach 1.4 and 55,000 feet — conditions it will fly when gathering community response data to its quiet sonic thump.

topComments.text: I remember as a kid thinking the X-15 was by far the coolest plane/rocket flying.

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
"The fascination that I have for test flying comes from being able to go into an environment where we have an idea what's going to happen, but then you have to be part of making it happen. And specifically for a mission like this one, it really is creating a stepping stone for the future."

Earlier this week, we announced the NASA Artemis III crew: commander NASA Astronaut Randy "Komrade" Bresnik, pilot Luca Parmitano, and mission specialists Andre Douglas and Astronaut Frank Rubio. Get to know them in this episode of Houston We Have a Podcast: https://www.nasa.gov/podcasts/houston-we-have-a-podcast/meet-artemis-iii/

media.ocrText: The four crew members of Artemis III -- from left to right, Luca Parmitano, Andre Douglas, Randy Bresnik, and Frank Rubio -- grin for the camera as they pose for a photo. They are wearing their orange flight suits against a white background; Luca and Randy are seated on stools, with Andre and Frank standing behind them. Credit: NASA/Helen Arase Vargas

topComments.text: Well, looks like the good ole boys are back.

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Could life be possible elsewhere in the Milky Way?

Soon, the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will launch to help answer this and other fundamental questions about our galaxy. Now, we’ve got a question for you: will you send your name on this epic journey?

Get your boarding pass and explore the universe with us: https://go.nasa.gov/RomanNames

media.ocrText: A photo of an individual in a clean room at NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center, wearing full clean room protective attire. They are holding a large, purple boarding pass that reads "Roman Telescope." Credit: NASA

topComments.text: The best proof of intelligent life elsewhere in our galaxy is that they are not coming here.

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration
International Space Station

sharedPost.text:
100K hours of holding space for science 🧪👩‍🚀
 
Yesterday, June 9, at 7:57 a.m. CT, the Expedition 74 crew called down to the Payload Operations Integration Center at NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center to highlight a major milestone for the International Space Station. The Microgravity Science Glovebox (MSG), which has taken part in hundreds of autonomous and crewed science experiments since its installation in 2002, has hit 100,000 hours of operation!

text:
Get ready for Earth joy!
 
Earlier today, we announced the four astronauts who will go to space as part of Artemis III. This mission will undertake a series of challenging tests in low Earth orbit in 2027, setting future NASA Artemis missions up to return humanity to the Moon. 

Learn more about Artemis III: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-iii/

text:
Introducing Artemis III. 

Four astronauts. Three launches. Two dockings. One splashdown. 

In 2027, the Artemis III mission will practice docking the Orion spacecraft with two lunar landers in low Earth orbit. On future Artemis missions, landers will bring astronauts to the lunar surface. While Artemis III will not land on the Moon, it will test the complex capabilities we need to return humanity there — this time to stay. 
 
NASA astronauts NASA Astronaut Randy "Komrade" Bresnik, Astronaut Frank Rubio, and Andre Douglas will join ESA - European Space Agency astronaut Luca Parmitano on this historic mission. https://go.nasa.gov/4xoS8MC

text:
Meet the astronauts of Artemis III!
We're providing an update on Artemis III, and we’ll introduce the mission’s four crew members.
During the test flight, the crew will lift off aboard NASA's Space Launch System rocket and remain in low Earth orbit where they’ll test our ability to meet and dock the Orion spacecraft with commercial human landers, operations which are needed to land astronauts on the Moon.

sharedPost.text:
The Moon joy didn’t stop after Artemis II — the science continued on Earth. 🌕✨
 
Teams are now analyzing postflight health data, organ chips from NASA’s AVATAR investigation, and a seven‑hour lunar observation campaign. 
Full story: https://go.nasa.gov/4ujPx3t

media.ocrText: An image of Victor Glover wearing a white spacesuit walks on a treadmill-like platform inside a large testing facility while attached to a support system that helps simulate lunar gravity. Engineers and technicians observe nearby as the astronaut tests mobility and movement capabilities for future Artemis missions. Blue structural frames, monitoring equipment, and cables surround the test area.

topComments.text: "Earth designs the tech, and the universe gives us the answers. Watching the legacy of Artemis II continue through groundbreaking biological and lunar research is incredibly inspiring. The road to Mars is being paved right now in these labs."

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Coming soon: one of history’s most complex missions 

Tune in on Tuesday, June 9, at 11 a.m. EDT (1500 UTC) to meet the astronauts flying aboard Artemis III, the mission that will test rendezvous and docking capabilities with commercial landers in low Earth orbit. Commercial landers are needed to bring astronauts to the lunar surface during future Artemis missions. 

Before Artemis IV heads back to the Moon, this third mission will be an important stepping stone for lunar landings during future Artemis missions. https://www.facebook.com/events/1526795995494848

text:
Journey to the center of the galaxy cluster

This spiral galaxy, known as Messier 88 or M88, can be found 63 million light-years from Earth in the Virgo Cluster, a collection of more than 1,000 galaxies held together by gravity. 

As its galaxies swirl and mix, M88 will spend hundreds of millions of years slowly drifting towards the innermost reaches of the Virgo Cluster. Along the way, it will get pushed and pulled by other galaxies in the cluster — reshaping it forever on its cosmic journey.

Learn more about this image from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope : https://go.nasa.gov/49Khni3

media.ocrText: A two-slide image depicting a bright spiral galaxy. Its wispy arms, brown and blue with reddish-pink dots, surround a bright, somewhat cloudy core. A few other galaxies are faintly visible around the borders of the image. Credit: ESA/Hubble & NASA, D. Thilker

✈️ JUST IN: The X-59 has gone supersonic! 

The X-59 achieved supersonic speeds for the first time ever today — a major milestone for NASA’s Quesst mission and an important step toward upcoming flights that will demonstrate its quiet supersonic technology ahead of future community overflights. 

Fast now. Quiet soon. 🔊➡️🤫 

Learn more: https://go.nasa.gov/4ulY4Dk

#NASA #X59 #Quesst

Our Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is officially slated to launch on Aug. 30!

Get the details and follow Roman's journey on our new Roman Space Telescope blog: https://go.nasa.gov/4a7f8FG

media.ocrText: An infographic about the Roman Space Telescope, titled "Capturing The Big Picture: What Roman Will Reveal." Various boxes beneath highlight that Roman will observe, for example, "80,000 supernovae" and "billions of galaxies". Credit: NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center

text:
Soon.

Later this year, our next space telescope will launch into orbit. The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will capture both the big picture and the finer details of the cosmos—observing distant celestial bodies with a field of view at least 100 times wider than Hubble’s.

Get the details on Roman and its mission: https://go.nasa.gov/4x5yDID

Add Roman to your phone—download this free poster: https://go.nasa.gov/4v4CfJH

media.ocrText: An illustration depicting the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope floating above an open book, being orbited by small satellites, telescopes, and celestial bodies. Behind Roman is a rosy stripe of stars and golden light. Credit: NASA/Jenny Mottar

text:
What's up for June?

The solstice brings summer to the Northern Hemisphere. Jupiter and Venus will appear close together in the night sky, and Mercury will join them soon after. The Moon will also pass in front of Venus on June 17. Learn more: https://go.nasa.gov/4ap33vB

text:
Which comes first, the galaxy or the black hole?

New data from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope shows that supermassive black holes can grow to their current size without a much larger host galaxy to feed them.

This helps to explain why some black holes in the early universe got so big so quickly. https://go.nasa.gov/3RQP9Mo

media.ocrText: An image of Abell 2744 (Pandora's Cluster) and Little Red Dot Abell2744-QSO1, captured by Webb's NIRCam, with compass arrows, scale bar, and color key for reference. At the bottom of the image is a color-coded list of the wavelengths of light that have been translated into visible-light colors. Credit: Image: NASA, ESA, CSA, Lukas Furtak (Ben-Gurion University); Image Processing: Alyssa Pagan (STScI)

topComments.text: The Black hole was first. Wears the light like a ring 💍 in the night, like Sauron…. ;)

user.name: NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration

text:
Soon... full moon!

Our next full moon will show up in the night skies on Sunday, May 31. Because it's the second full moon we've had this month, it's also known as a Blue Moon.

If the skies are clear in your neighborhood, this may be a great chance to take your new favorite Moon photo. Whether you're using a professional camera or just your smartphone, we have a Lunar Photography Guide with tips and tricks for shooting the Moon. (For example: if you're on your phone, consider using a photo timer to keep from jostling your phone when you snap the picture.)

Check out the full guide: https://science.nasa.gov/moon/photography-guide/

media.ocrText: A close-up photo of the Moon, which is about three-quarters full; its left side is in shadow. Its gray seas and circular impact craters are clearly visible. Near the center of the image, the International Space Station can just barely be seen passing in front of the Moon. Credit: NASA/Joel Kowsky
A reddish Moon, with clouds passing in front of it, floats above a quickly-moving subway train at night. Credit: NASA/Bill Ingalls
A large, golden Moon, partially shrouded by light clouds, hangs above the peak of the Washington Monument, which is covered in scaffolding. Credit: NASA/Bill Ingalls

NASA's Hubble Space Telescope

sharedPost.text:
Stealing centerstage ⭐

Foreground stars, recognizable by their diffraction spikes, shine in front of the dwarf irregular galaxy ESO 490-017.

Diffraction spikes form when an intense source of light interacts with the four struts inside Hubble that support its secondary mirror. As light passes around these struts and recombines on the other side, it creates four bright spikes around the bright source of light.

Hubble studied ESO 490-017 to learn more about the movement of galaxies and galaxy clusters through space. This particular galaxy is about 23 million light-years away: https://go.nasa.gov/4e8sCmU

Image credit: NASA, ESA, R. Tully (University of Hawaii); Image Processing: G. Kober (NASA/Catholic University of America)

media.ocrText: A foreground star at image center shines brightly with four diffraction spikes. Above this star is a diffuse and faint dwarf irregular galaxy. The galaxy itself has bright blue and red stars.

text:
On Tuesday, June 9, we’ll announce the four astronauts who will orbit Earth aboard the NASA Artemis III mission!

Watch our live event at 11 a.m. EDT (1500 UTC) to learn about the crew and their mission to test rendezvous and docking capabilities between Orion and commercial human landing systems — a critical step needed to deliver astronauts to the surface of the Moon. https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-to-announce-artemis-iii-crew-provide-mission-progress-update/

media.ocrText: The Artemis II mission patch is seen on the right shoulder of four orange Orion Crew Survival System suits on Jan. 17, 2026, in the suit-up room of the Neil A. Armstrong Operations and Checkout Building at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. The mission patch contains imagery of the Moon and the Earth. Credit: NASA/Joel Kowsky
